data.source: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (state-level CO2 emissions), U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics (average weekly working hours); additional covariate data from Bureau of Economic Analysis, U.S. Census Bureau, League of Conservation Voters
data.wave_or_subset: State-year panel for all 50 U.S. states, 2007–2013
data.notes: Dependent variable is fossil-fuel CO2 emissions; key independent variable is average weekly working hours. Dataset compiled from multiple public sources.

method.description: Researchers estimate the relationship between average weekly working hours and state-level carbon dioxide emissions, controlling for political, economic, and demographic factors. They employ panel regression models with corrections for autocorrelation and panel heteroskedasticity.
steps: Assemble state-year dataset (2007–2013) combining CO2 emissions, working hours, and control variables.
Estimate two-way fixed-effects Prais-Winsten models with panel-corrected standard errors (Models 1 & 2).
Estimate random-effects Prais-Winsten models (Models 3 & 4) for comparison.
Estimate hybrid models with AR(1) correction to separate within- and between-state effects (Models 5 & 6).
Interpret coefficients for scale and composition effects of working hours on emissions.
Conduct robustness checks for regional dummies and environmentalism controls.
method.models: Two-way fixed-effects Prais-Winsten regression; random-effects Prais-Winsten regression; hybrid (within–between) panel models with AR(1) correction and panel-corrected standard errors
method.outcome_variable: State-level carbon dioxide emissions from fossil-fuel combustion
method.independent_variables: Average weekly working hours per worker (log form)
method.control_variables: GDP per hour, percent employed population, total population, energy production, manufacturing share of GDP, average household size, percent working-age population, census-region dummies, state environmentalism score

results.summary: Across multiple panel regression specifications, longer average weekly working hours are significantly and positively associated with higher state-level CO2 emissions. A 1 % increase in working hours raises emissions by roughly 0.55 %–0.68 %, controlling for economic scale, industrial composition, population, energy production, and environmental policy indicators. State environmentalism reduces emissions, while population size and energy production increase them.

numerical_results.outcome_name: CO2 emissions (scale effect, Model 1 fixed effects)
numerical_results.unit: percent change in emissions per 1 % change in working hours

numerical_results.outcome_name: CO2 emissions (composition effect, Model 2 fixed effects)
numerical_results.unit: percent change in emissions per 1 % change in working hours

numerical_results.outcome_name: CO2 emissions (scale effect, Model 3 random effects)
numerical_results.unit: percent change in emissions per 1 % change in working hours

numerical_results.outcome_name: CO2 emissions (composition effect, Model 4 random effects)
numerical_results.unit: percent change in emissions per 1 % change in working hours
